American professional baseball player who in 1968 and 1969 led the National League (NL) in home runs and runs batted in.
American business and sports executive who became principal owner of the New York Yankees baseball team in 1973.
Sports spectacle involving conflict between a bull and one or more contestants that fought in an outdoor arena according to certain rules and procedures.
American ice skater who was the  winner of an unprecedented five gold medals in speed skating at the 1980 Winter Olympics and  who later became a cyclist.
Breed of sporting dog dating back to the 19th century and that probably derives from crossing the retrieving setter with the Irish water spaniel.
Breed of spaniel known in Great Britain for several centuries and popular throughout the world because of its endurance and ability to stand extremes of heat and cold.
Breed of dog that originated in Scotland about 1865 from a cross between a yellow wavy-coated retriever and a tweed water spaniel.
American track-and-field athlete who was the first American woman to win three track-and-field gold medals at a single Olympic Games.
American baseball player known as Joltin' Joe, who is considered one of the greatest hitters and center fielders of all time.
American baseball player and executive who helped organize baseball's American League.
American professional baseball player, who led the National League in strikeouts four times (1932-1935).
American sportswriter known as the Voice of Sports during the 1920s and who is considered a pioneer in the development of modern sports journalism.
Racing contest of homemade, gravity-propelled or coasting cars for drivers aged 11 to 15 , held annually since 1934 in Akron, Ohio.
American football player who was the first two-time winner of the Heisman Trophy, an award given annually to the best player in college football.
American professional automobile racer, a four-time winner of the prestigious Indianapolis 500 automobile race.
Athletic endurance competition involving three sports, usually swimming, cycling, and long-distance running.
Either of two dog breeds, the German shorthaired pointer, or kurzhaar, and the German wirehaired pointer, or drahthaar.
American professional tennis player who was the winner of the French Open in 1991 and 1992 and the Australian Open in 1992 and 1993.
American professional tennis player who in 1991, at the age of 15, became the youngest woman to reach the semifinal round at Wimbledon.
American professional baseball player famous for a double-play combination with John Evers and Frank Chance.
American professional baseball player and manager, considered one of the greatest right-handed batters.
American professional baseball player famous for a double-play combination with Joseph Tinker and Frank Chance.
American professional baseball player nicknamed the Iron Man because of his endurance--five times in the course of his career he pitched two games in a single day.
American professional baseball player, a great pitcher, who retired in 1993 with the major league record for most career strikeouts and most career no-hitters.
Breed of working dog that originated centuries ago in that part of far northeastern Russia inhabited by a people known as the Chukchi.
American authority on the card game of bridge; he was born of American parents in Poyana de Vervilao, Romania.
Dog, breed that originated in Scotland several centuries ago but was not bred in its present form until the middle of the 19th century in England and Scotland.
American basketball player, coach, sports executive, and broadcaster who was one of the greatest players to play the guard position in the NBA.
American professional baseball player, one of the best second basemen in the sport, celebrated for his heavy hitting and sure, graceful fielding.
Breed of toy dog that reputedly originated in China many centuries ago and was later introduced in Japan, where it became the pet of royalty.
Breed of toy dog bred in England from a number of other terriers, principally the Skye and the Manchester terriers.
American football player and coach at the University of Notre Dame who revolutionized football strategy.
American professional baseball player known as the Iron Horse because he played in a record-setting 2130 games in succession from 1925 to 1939.
American boxer who became the welterweight and middleweight champion of the world, and who is considered one of the greatest boxers in history.
Breed of working dog that exists in four varieties--the Groenendael, Laekenois, Malinois, and Tervuren.
Breed of toy dog, which reputedly originated in Japan in ancient times and was then introduced to China, Spain and England.
American golfer who is a four-time winner of the Ladies Professional Golf Association player of the year award (1978, 1979, 1985, 1988).
Breed of working dog of unusually large size and statuesque appearance that supposedly originated in Germany about the middle of the 16th century.
American football authority who invented the system of downs, created the position of quarterback, and set the number of players at 11.
Canadian ice hockey player, nicknamed The Great One, who is the National Hockey League career leader in goals, assists, and points.
American football player, a quarterback, noted for his ball-passing, physical agility, and for engineering last-minute comebacks.
American basketball player who was voted by the Associated Press as the outstanding college basketball player of the first half of the 20th century.
Coach of the United States basketball team that won gold medals at the 1964 and 1968 Summer Olympics and who was elected to the Basketball Hall of Fame in 1968.
American tennis player who at the age of 41 played a historic 112-game match at Wimbledon in 1969, eight years after he had announced his retirement from tennis.
American scholar and baseball official, ruler on the lifetime suspension of Pete Rose from professional baseball.
American automobile racer, winner of 200 National Association of Stock Car Auto Racing (NASCAR) events.
American professional automobile racer who won the Indianapolis 500 four times and retired in 1992 following a crash during training..
American professional boxer whose nickname, the Manassa Mauler, is derived from his birthplace and his aggressive style as a fighter.
Various leisure-time activities that usually involve collecting objects such as stamps or butterflies or creating objects such as wood carvings or embroidery.
American pro basketball player who became a center for the New York Knicks in 1985 and was a member of the U.S. 1992 Olympic gold-medal basketball team.
Breed of working dog whose ancestors probably came from Central Asia or Siberia; fossil remains date its appearance in Europe between 1800 and 1000 B.C..
American tennis player, winner of the men's singles title at Wimbledon (1992), the United States Open (1994), and the Australian Open (1995).
American professional baseball player whose career total of 573 home runs ranks him second only to Babe Ruth in the American League.
Pseudonym of Jean François Gravelet (1824-97), French acrobat and tightrope walker, who was born in Saint-Omer.
American professional baseball player who starred as a catcher for the National League's Brooklyn Dodgers from 1948 to 1957.
American tennis player whose intense competitiveness and outspoken support for better treatment of women players were instrumental in increasing the popularity of women's tennis.
Series of organized sports events held annually in Scotland, and in many places in Canada and the United States, since the 19th century.
American basketball coach who in 1966 became the coach with the most regular season wins in the history of the National Basketball Association.
Puerto Rican professional baseball player who was the first Hispanic player elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame (1973) and the second player to be honored on a United States postage stamp.
Czech-born tennis player, three-time winner of both the French Open  and the U.S. Open, who in 1980 led Czechoslovakia to its first Davis Cup victory.
American baseball player and manager who is noted as a colorful and outspoken figure and is considered one of the most successful managers in major league baseball.
British golfer for whom a trophy awarded annually by the Professional Golfers' Association of America for low-average scoring is named.
American professional football executive who was commissioner of the National Football League (NFL) from 1960 to 1989.
American football player and motion picture actor who led the National Football League in rushing yards eight times during his nine-year career.
American basketball player who, by the time he retired in 1992, was considered by many experts as the greatest all-around player in basketball history.
American boxer who in 1986 won the World Boxing Council heavyweight title, becoming the youngest heavyweight fighter ever to win a world title.

American baseball player noted for the length of his career and the speed and variety of his pitches, such as the hesitation ball, which involved stopping his pitching motion momentarily during a pitch.
British corporation director and automobile racer, born in Chislehurst, Kent, England and educated in Uppingham and abroad.
American baseball player known as the Say Hey Kid who is considered by many to be the greatest baseball player in history.
American football player, coach, and owner of the Chicago Bears who retired from professional football coaching in 1968 with six championships and more wins than any other coach.
American baseball player, a left-handed pitcher known for his screwball, who struck out five consecutive batters during the 1934 All-Star game.
American professional football player who was quarterback for the Green Bay Packers through five NFL championships and the team's first two Super Bowl titles.
American football player, a quarterback, whose success with the Cleveland Browns of the AFC after World War II helped merge the Browns into the NFL in 1950.
Versatile football player, a Green Bay Packer for his whole career, who was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1986.
American professional baseball player nicknamed the Rocket who was a three-time winner of the American League's Cy Young Award.
American baseball executive who developed the farm system, whereby major league teams own controlling interests in minor league clubs in order to obtain promising young players.
American professional football coach and player who in 1993 became the winningest coach in the history of the National Football League.
American professional baseball player with 534 career home runs (ranking ninth best in the major leagues), who is only the third player in American League history to win the Triple Crown.
Breed of dog developed more than 100 years ago by crossing the English bulldog with the now-extinct white English terrier, and the resulting type with the Spanish pointer.
American professional football player, a quarterback, who in 1995 set NFL career records in passing completions, passing yardage, touchdown passes, and passing attempts.
American professional football player who was voted the most valuable player of the National Football League in 1959, 1964, and 1967.
American professional baseball player and manager who, in three trips overseas as manager of groups of players, introduced the game of baseball to Europe and eastern Asia.
Russian chess grand master and world champion who was born in Moscow and educated at the universities of Petrograd (now Saint Petersburg) and Paris.
Breed of working dog that originated in Europe sometime before the Christian era, probably from a large Asian dog brought to Europe by Roman soldiers.
American professional baseball player, manager, and owner who became manager of the Philadelphia Athletics in 1901 and led the team to five world championships.
American professional football player, a versatile running back for the Chicago Bears, who was elected to the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 1993.

Breed of terrier that supposedly originated two centuries ago but was not recognized as a separate breed until 1884; until that time the dog was known either by its present name or as the Old English terrier.
American track athlete born in Los Angeles, California and educated at the University of California at Los Angeles.
American sports administrator and entrepreneur born in Evanston, Illinois and educated at San José State University.
American professional baseball player, the first pitcher to win the Cy Young Award in both the American (1972) and National (1978) leagues.
American professional automobile racer, winner of more than 50 Indy Car races, the Indianapolis 500, and the World Grand Prix Formula One championship.
American professional baseball player nicknamed the Flying Dutchman who is considered one of the greatest shortstops in the history of baseball.
A favorite gymnast at the 1976 Olympic Games in Montreal. She won three gold medals and a bronze medal in individual competition, and a silver medal for the Romanian team's second-place finish.
Breed of working dog that originated in Apolda, Germany, where it was first bred about 1890 from the German shepherd, the Rottweiler, the black and tan terrier, and the German pinscher.
American baseball player and manager known as The Splendid Splinter who is considered one of the greatest hitters and outfielders in major league history.
American boxer, heavyweight champion from 1978 to 1985, whose comeback in the early 1990s led to an unsuccessful challenge against title-holder Evander Holyfield in 1992.
American baseball player who started in the American League in 1907 with the Boston Red Sox and became one of the finest outfielders and hitters in baseball history.
American professional football player, a quarterback, who led the NFL's San Francisco 49ers to Super Bowl victories in 1982, 1985, 1989, and 1990.
American basketball coach who achieved his greatest fame as coach of the Tar Heels of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill.
American basketball player, a leading scorer in the National Basketball Association, who led the Chicago Bulls to four NBA championships.
American basketball coach and player who starred in the American Basketball League, where he played center for both the Original Celtics of New York City and the Cleveland Rosenblums.
American professional baseball player, one of the most gifted left-hand pitchers in baseball history, especially noted for his speed and strikeout records.
American professional baseball player, famous member of a double-play combination with Joseph Tinker and John Evers, born in Fresno, California.
Breed of shorthaired cat, originally from the vicinity of the city of Arkhangel'sk, once known variously as Spanish Blue, Archangel and Maltese.
American professional basketball player who achieved his greatest fame as a college basketball player at Louisiana State University in Baton Rouge.
American professional baseball player, one of the greatest right-handed pitchers in baseball, born in Factoryville, Pennsylvania, and educated at Bucknell University.
